payload.intro: 引用 ICOMOS 结论部分对价值标准的判断摘要。

criteria_en.ii: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated serial property exhibits an important interchange of human values in the creation and construction of Xiongnu noble tombs that reflect influences from the funeral cultures of the Han Dynasty, the Nannang (Nangnang), and the Altai. The archaeological artefacts found in the burials, including lacquered cups and utensils created for the royal court of the Han Dynasty in China, embroidered textiles from Persia, stone and glass beads crafted in Central Asia, Greco-Roman silverware, and Roman glassware, show that the Xiongnu were directly involved in trade and cultural relations between the Eastern and Western worlds from the 3 rd century BCE to the 2nd century CE. ICOMOS considers that the tomb construction as well as the foreign artefacts that accompanied the dead indicate important external contacts of the Xiongnu. However, participation in information and trade networ ks is very common among cultures and cannot be considered an outstanding trait. The role the Xiongnu played in fostering trade along the Steppe Route of the Silk Roads is highlighted, connecting the Eastern and Western worlds, but the artefacts in the tombs only establish the participation of the Xiongnu in this trade; they do not define the role they played and the influence they had on other cultures. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated.
criteria_en.iii: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that The Cemetery Complexes of the Xiongnu Nobility testify in an exceptional way to the extinct Xiongnu funerary culture. It provides insights into the burial 147 customs, rituals, established traditions and tomb construction of the nomadic Xiongnu nobles. Even though there exists already more than a century of research on these tombs, much about the Xiongnu funerary culture, with its burial customs and rituals, is still unknown or not well understood. Nevertheless, ICOMOS considers that the tombs with their deep burial chambers, satellite graves and sacrificial structures, as well as with their rich offerings, make the nominated serial property an exceptional testimony to the extinct Xiongnu funerary culture. ICOMOS considers that The Cemetery Complexes of the Xiongnu Nobility are an exceptional expression of, and the richest source of insight into, the functioning of this culture of the Central Asian Steppe. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is demonstrated.
criteria_en.iv: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated serial property is the best extant example of a specific type of Central Asian funerary structure from the rd century BCE to the 2nd century CE. ICOMOS considers that the size and especially the depth (over twenty metres) of some of the tombs are proof of the technical and organisational capabilities of Xiongnu society. The use terraces and the dromoi made construction in sandy soils possible. However, the details and the evolution of the construction process require further study, and ICOMOS considers that the construction process remains not documented enough for the tombs to be considered an outstanding example of a typology of building or technological ensemble. Furthermore, no significant stage in human history that would be illustrated by the nominated property has been clearly defined. Finally, the description of the tomb construction as a cultural tradition justifies better criterion (iii). ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated. ICOMOS considers that the Xiongnu nobility burials, which include rich grave offerings as well as satellite graves and sacrificial structures, include attributes that document the Xiongnu burial culture well. Many of the known graves have not yet been excavated and represent a significant opportunity for future investigation. The selection of component parts testifies to the extension of the Xiongnu funerary culture throughout the empire and to the economic power of the nomadic Xiongnu society. ICOMOS con siders that the nominated property meets criterion (iii), but that criterion (ii) and (iv) have not been demonstrated.

recommendation_criteria_en.iii: The Cemetery Complexes of the Xiongnu Nobility testif y in an exceptional way to the extinct Xiongnu funerary culture. They provide insights into the burial customs, rituals, established traditions and tomb construction of the nomadic Xiongnu nobles. Even though there exists already more than a century of research on these tombs, much about the Xiongnu funerary culture, with its burial customs and rituals, is still unknown or not well understood. These tombs with their deep burial chambers, satellite graves and sacrificial structures, as well as with their rich offerings, make the serial property an exceptional testimony to the extinct Xiongnu funerary culture. The serial property is an exceptional expression of, and the richest source of insight into, the functioning of this culture of the Central Asian Steppe.

criteria_en.ii: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that Wat Phra Mahathat remarkably exhibits religious diversity, spiritual continuity and vital elements that capture the waves, art forms and essence of important religions such as Hinduism, Mahayana Buddhism and Theravada Buddhism, which have been transmitted across the south of maritime Asia for about 1,500 years. The architectural forms, Buddhist practices and living traditions of Wat Phra Mahathat also had a significant impact on other places in Thailand, Malaysia and beyond. ICOMOS considers that to justify this criterion, the fusion of the ideas or values should be deemed exceptional and influential over time or within a specific cultural area. While the Great Reliquary has demonstrated the exceptionality of the fusion of ideas, its lasting inf luence has not been adequately established. In the additional information of November 2025, the State Party provided examples of stupas constructed in the subsequent dynasties in other regions of Thailand, Myanmar and Malaysia that were influenced by the Great Reliquary. Based on the evidence provided by the State Party, ICOMOS considers that this criterion is demonstrated.
criteria_en.vi: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that Wat Phra Mahathat is the spiritual centre of the region, with the living traditions of diverse belief systems as witnessed in daily practices, merit makings and annual ceremonies, including the annual clothwrapping ceremony for the Great Reliquary, the shamanistic Nora dance, ancestor worship, Brahminic rituals, rare artworks and local chronicles. These traditions vividly represent the 159 relationships between this sacred site and its surrounding communities and inspire other monasteries and communities in Thailand and beyond. ICOMOS considers that to justify this criterion, the associated traditions should be of outstanding universal significance. ICOMOS acknowledges the association of the numerous living traditions, and notes that the Nora dance and the chronicles are of outstanding universal significance. ICOMOS therefore considers that this criterion has been demonstrated. ICOMOS considers that, in addition to the attributes identified by the State Party, the overall layout of the monastery, the spatial arrangement of the religious buildings, and the views to and from the Great Reliquary are also attributes that convey the proposed Outstanding Universal Value. ICOMOS considers that the nominated property meets criteria (ii) and (vi).

criteria.ii: ICOMOS认为标准 ii 已得到证明。
criteria.vi: ICOMOS认为标准 vi 已得到证明。

recommendation_criteria_en.ii: Wat Phra Mahathat is an exceptional testament to the interchange of human values. The Great Reliquary demonstrates the influence of Brahmanism, Hinduism and the Mahayana and Theravada schools of Buddhism, originating from Pala art in Nalanda, Central Javanese art, Sri Lankan art and Mon art from southern Myanmar. In turn, the combined architectural style of the Great Reliquary has exerted a profound influence on subsequent religious buildings in Thailand, Malaysia and beyond.
recommendation_criteria_en.vi: As the testimony to diverse belief systems and the spiritual centre of Nakhon Si Thammarat, Wat Phra Mahathat is the heart of living religious traditions with artistic and literary works of outstanding universal significance, such as the Nora dance drama and the local chronicles. These traditions vividly represent the relationships between this sacred site and its surrounding communities and inspired other monasteries and communities in Thailand and beyond.

criteria_en.ii:
This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the Modernist architecture of Tashkent represents a significant interchange of human values in technology, planning, and design. The city acted as a crossroads where global modernist trends, such as prefabrication and curtain walls, were reinterpreted through the lens of local cultural, social, and environmental contexts. This exchange was facilitated by the movement of architects and engineers from across the USSR, particularly during post-earthquake reconstruction. The resulting “Tashkent model” created a regional variant of modernism that was later shared trans -nationally, influencing urban development and socialist international cooperation in other non-Western nations, particularly in Africa and nonaligned countries.

ICOMOS considers that, whil st the urban and architectural reconstruction experiment in Tashkent may have influenced other cities and capitals across the Soviet republics, it is much less evident how, and to what extent, Tashkent could be seen as a model that directly influenced urban and architectural developments in nonaligned countries. Substantial further research would be needed to justify this criterion from this perspective. The fusion of modernist architectural language with local traditional motifs and craftsmanship is not specific to Tashkent; it can be found elsewhere in the USSR and in specific manifestations of modernism globally. Therefore, this interchange would not suffice to justify this criterion.

ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated.
criteria_en.iv:
This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated serial property is an outstanding example of a modernist urban landscape representing a significant stage in history: the era of post -war socialist modernisation. It illustrates a nonWestern model of development designed to demonstrate progress and equality outside of capitalist frameworks. The “ensembles” of the city integrate monumental public buildings, infrastructure, and green networks into a unified system that structured daily life according to socialist values. These structures represent the technological and ideological peak of lateSoviet architecture, particularly through advancements in seismic -resistant design and industrialised prefabrication within a regional context.

ICOMOS considers that Tashkent Modernist Architecture outstandingly illustrates developments of architecture and urban planning during the post-Thaw period in a Soviet socialist society. The series of the nominated urban component parts 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and represents an outstanding ensemble of urban foci that, consciously combining modernist themes and aspirations with reinterpreted local motifs and building patterns, has contributed to shaping the modernist urban form of Tashkent. Tashkent Modernist Architecture is one of the finest examples of architectural and urban design, reflecting, in an exceptional and specific manner, the broader movement of “localised modernism ” spread across the Soviet realm, developed within the cultural and climatic environment of Central Asia.  ICOMOS considers that, in addition to the attributes identified in the nomination dossier, urban, visual and functional links among the component parts are also essential in conveying the proposed Outstanding Universal Value and criteria. Landscaping and urban vegetation in the broader urban setting also contribute to understanding and appreciation of Central Asian urban and architectural modernism of Tashkent. ICOMOS considers it important to identify specific tangible attributes for each component part to ensure they are taken into account in management and conservation actions. In the October 2025 additional information, the State Party also provided a table that explicitly shows how each component part contributes to illustrating each attribute.

Tashkent Modernist Architecture. Modernity and Tradition in Central Asia outstandingly illustrates developments in architecture and urban planning during the post-Thaw period in a Soviet socialist society. Designed as prominent nodes within the city, the component parts, consciously combining modernist themes and aspirations with reinterpreted local motifs and building patterns, formed cohesive ensembles that shaped the form and identity of the city, integrated with the urban fabric, served as landmarks, and enhanced urban perspectives. The serial property exemplifies the combination of Soviet standardisation and site- specific designs. Monumental art further enriched its expression, blending abstract aesthetics with ideological themes of progress and cultural identity. The serial property is one of the finest examples of architectural and urban design, reflecting, in an exceptional and specific manner, the broader movement of “localised modernism” that spread across the Soviet realm, developed within the cultural and climatic environment of Central Asia.

recommendation_criteria_en.iv: Tashkent Modernist Architecture. Modernity and Tradition in Central Asia outstandingly illustrates developments in architecture and urban planning during the post-Thaw period in a Soviet socialist society. Designed as prominent nodes within the city, the component parts, consciously combining modernist themes and aspirations with reinterpreted local motifs and building patterns, formed cohesive ensembles that shaped the form and identity of the city, integrated with the urban fabric, served as landmarks, and enhanced urban perspectives. The serial property exemplifies the combination of Soviet standardisation and site- specific designs. Monumental art further enriched its expression, blending abstract aesthetics with ideological themes of progress and cultural identity. The serial property is one of the finest examples of architectural and urban design, reflecting, in an exceptional and specific manner, the broader movement of “localised modernism” that spread across the Soviet realm, developed within the cultural and climatic environment of Central Asia.

payload.intro: 引用 ICOMOS 结论部分对价值标准的判断摘要。

criteria_en.ii: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated property contributes in an exceptional way to the th-century modernisation of society by integrating a new international language and traditional national ideas in both architecture and design to meet the needs of individuals, families, communities and society, serving as a symbol of Finland’s independence while integrating scientific progress with a distinct human dimension. Aalto’s approach prioriti ses the sensory human experience through tactile materials (wood, brick, copper), unique forms, and thoughtful lighting. The designs deeply integrate locality, climate, and seasonal cycles, often dissolving boundaries between indoor and outdoor spaces to enhance well -being. Rooted in individual experience, this method combines art and technology to create functional, high-quality environments that foster citizenship, community and solidarity. The Aalto Works, as a manifestation of a collective design, stand out as a living legacy where innovative mass production meets traditional craftsmanship, establishing an enduring tradition of empathetic and sustainable architecture. To justify this criterion, the tangible attributes of a nominated property must demonstrate that ideas or values about developments in architecture, technology, monumental ar ts, town -planning or landscape design were exported or imported and transformed exceptionally, or that they reflect a local fusion of ideas/values that could be deemed exceptional and influential over time or within a specific cultural area. ICOMOS considers that the nominated serial property comprises representative modern buildings, ensembles, and sites that significantly influenced the formation and development of modernism, and which continue to impact contemporary architecture worldwide. Through an organic functionalist approach, the Aalto Works adapted global Modern Movement ideas to local contexts, seamlessly integrating exteriors with interiors and harmoni sing with their settings. This architecture offers an outstanding response to universal societal needs, prioriti sing citizen well-being throughout the moderni sation process. Bringing together modern concepts and building traditions, this collective design approach represents outstanding responses to various universal societal needs of individuals and communities, through the introduction of a new language of form, materials, and production methods, executed with exceptional sensitivity, simplicity, and integrity. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is demonstrated. 189 ICOMOS considers that the nominated property meets criterion (ii).

recommendation_criteria_en.ii: Through an organic functionalist approach, the Aalto Works adapted global Modern Movement ideas to local contexts, seamlessly integrating exteriors with interiors and harmonising with their settings. This architecture offers an outstanding response to universal societal needs, prioritising citizen well-being throughout the modernisation process. By bridging modern concepts with building traditions, the collective design approach introduced a new language of form, materiality, and production methods , executed with exceptional sensitivity, simplicity, and integrity.

payload.intro: 引用 ICOMOS 结论部分对价值标准的判断摘要。

criteria_en.iv: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated property offers an outstanding example of the confrontation between two systems, a defensive one represented by the Atlantic Wall, and an offensive one through the maritime assault prepared by the Allies; a confrontation whose scale and intensity are exceptional in history. The landscape of the DDay Landing Beaches also illustrates, through preserved vestiges, military cemeteries, and commemorative monuments, the immediate desire to convey the historical significance of this event. ICOMOS considers that the proposed justification for this criterion raises two fundamental issues. The first concerns the insufficient number of tangible and immovable attributes. The proposed attributes included within the nominated property that reflect the defensive system are very limited in comparison with those that remain along the Normandy coast. Elements reflecting the offensive system are mostly mobile and were not intended to be permanent. Furthermore, they are not subject to conservation measures. Natural elements have also undergone significant changes in certain areas of the nominated property due to ongoing processes, such as erosion and debris accumulation. The exceptional scale and intensity of the confrontation would not necessarily suggest Outstanding Universal Value. The second issue is linked to the fact the proposed justification for this criterion is more associative than typological, which does not align with the definition underpinning the narrative for this criterion. Consequently, the justification put forward by the State Party fails to demonstrate that the nominated property offers “an outstanding example of a type of building, architectural or technological ensemble or landscape”. 203 These concerns were raised in the ICOMOS interim report. In the additional information of February 2026, the State Party decided to withdrew this criterion, to focus only on the associative and memorial dimensions of the nominated property.
criteria_en.vi: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated property stands as a symbol of immense hope and progress towards freedom. Long awaited by those oppressed under the Nazi yoke across Europe, the D-Day Landings marked a turning point in this struggle; they illustrate how a collective alliance of nations committed to these values established a lasting peace, whose universal significance remains embedded in this landscape. ICOMOS considers that this memorial construct raises significant questions. Firstly, ICOMOS considers that messages of peace cannot constitute the primary argument to justify a potential Outstanding Universal Value, insofar as the aspiration for peace or freedom cannot be measured or compared with that of other sites. While these messages and values are facets of many sites of memory associated with recent conflicts, it is nonetheless impossible to compare them to determine which messages of peace or freedom possess outstanding universal significance and which do not. ICOMOS considers that these messages and values pertain more to the process of reconciliation than to the proposed Outstanding Universal Value. Furthermore, the justification for criterion (vi) must “be directly or tangibly associated with events or living traditions, with ideas, or with beliefs, with artistic and literary works ” expressing outstanding universal significance, which must therefore be established. In this regard, ICOMOS finds it difficult to assess the exceptionality of the D-Day Landings in isolation without situating this event within a broader global context. The Second World War was an event of outstanding universal significance, and Operation Neptune took place within this complex context, as evidenced by the multiplicity of theatres of operation in various countries worldwide, some of which were also decisive for the outcome of this global conflict. However, the nomination dossier appears to establish an implicit equivalence among the liberation of France following the Normandy Landings, that of Europe, and the end of the war itself. This reasoning obscures other operations of crucial importance. For example, the liberation of Berlin by the Red Army occurred almost a year later, in May 1945, as well as the battles on nonEuropean fronts, such as the defeat of Japan in August 1945. While t he Normandy Landings were undoubtedly a decisive turning point in the Second World War, they were nevertheless part of a series of interconnected operations resulting from cooperation between the Allies across several theatres of operation. Recognising this complexity and the plurality of historical experiences within the justification for criterion (vi) appears essential to justify this criterion while valuing the symbolic dimension of the nominated property. Finally, ICOMOS notes that the nomination dossier does not provide an in-depth reflection on the evolution of postwar commemoration and the changes it has undergone over the decades. The challenge also lies in recognising the diversity of memories, thereby avoiding the limits of universalisation, which would in reality amount to applying one reading of the Second World War. On the basis of these complexities ICOMOS requested in its interim report whether a revision to the proposed justification for this criterion could be envisaged. In the additional information of February 2026, the State Party provided further details on the context of the D-Day Landings associated with the nominated property, and complementary reflections on memorialisation, alongside a revised justification focused solely on criterion (vi). ICOMOS considers that this additional information contributes to provide a better contextualised explanation of the memorialisation process, recognising its complexities and multi vocality. However, the proposed revised justification for criterion (vi) shifts away from the association with the historical event itself and its memorialisation, extending well beyond these aspects to assign political meaning and associations to it. The revised justification presents the nominated property as associated not only with the Normandy D -Day Landings but also, by extension, with the foundation of the United Nations. ICOMOS considers that the proposed Outstanding Universal Value of the nominated property cannot be focused only on abstract notions, such as hope, associated with the D -Day Landings. It further considers that the proposed revised justification for criterion (vi) makes an unwarranted leap in attempting to establish a direct and tangible association between the nominated property, the foundation of the United Nations and the resulting post-war international order, as such links are not credibly expressed through its attributes. Conversely, ICOMOS recognises that, having largely retained their appearance from the Second World War, the Normandy Beaches constitute a natural memorial capable of evoking powerful memories of the D -Day Landings. As part of a sequence of pivotal operations across multiple theatres in Eastern and Southern Europe, North Africa, and the AsiaPacific region, reflecting unprecedented multinational cooperation and deployment of resources, the DDay Landings marked the start of a long and costly campaign to liberate northwestern Europe from the yoke of the Third Reich and ultimately contributed to the end of the war in Europe. 204 ICOMOS considers that this criterion is demonstrated based on the abovementioned revised justification. ICOMOS concurs with the attributes identified by the State Party but also considers that some geographical and landscape features, along with surviving defensive and offensive remains across the wider coastal stretch and buffer zone, further reinforce the memorial associations of the nominated property. ICOMOS considers that the nominated property meets criterion (vi) based on the revised justification proposed in this evaluation text. Criterion (iv) was withdrawn by the State Party in February 2026.

criteria.iv: ICOMOS认为标准 iv 已得到证明。
criteria.vi: ICOMOS认为标准 vi 已得到证明。

recommendation_criteria_en.vi: The Beaches of the D -Day Landings , Normandy, 1944 are a site of memory directly and materially associated with the event known as the “Normandy Landings”, which took place in 1944 during the Second World War . Operation Neptune, launched on 6 June 1944 on the Normandy coast, was the first phase of Operation Overlord, designed to open a new front in Western Europe against the troops of the Third Reich, one of the decisive moments in the Second World War, which marked the beginning of the end of the war in Western Europe. The Beaches of the D -Day Landings outstandingly and tangibly embody, symbolically commemorate and evoke memories of the concerted, multinational collaboration and coordination of logistical and military efforts, and the immense human sacrifice required to break an entrenched authoritarian occupation in Europe in the mid- 20 th century and paved the way for reconstruction, reconciliation, and a renewed commitment to collective security. I ntegrity The serial property includes all the component parts necessary to express its Outstanding Universal Value, particularly its associative value. The series incorporates the beaches themselves, as landing and amphibious assault sites on 6 June 1944; remains of the logistical facilities used for the offensive; remains of the defensive system or “Atlantic Wall” that the Allies faced; traces left by the confrontation in the landscape, deliberately preserved; commemorative sites; memorial monuments; and finally, a military cemetery that provides a representation of other military cemeteries. The individual integrity of each component part varies, but all nevertheless present a sufficient set of attributes to fully express the associative value of the serial property. The landscape integrity of the serial property, although vulnerable, can be described as satisfactory as it is adequately preserved despite pressures, including urban and tourism development on some component parts. Threats related to the natural evolution of the coastline and from facilities supporting the transition to renewable energy should also be closely monitored.

entries_en.serial_selection: The serial nominated property includes all the component parts necessary to express its proposed Outstanding Universal Value, particularly its associative significance.

criteria_en.ii: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated serial property represents an exceptional milestone in the process of diffusion of Capetian military architecture in the 13 th-14th centuries, from Western Europe towards the Baltic and the Danube River regions, as well as the Mediterranean Middle East. The ensemble of fortifications, characterised by architectural cohesion and skilful adaptation of technical solutions to the landscape setting, represents an architectural model, which spread beyond the borders of the seneschalty. Its territorial organisation influenced the geopolitical developments in the wider region. ICOMOS considers that the system of fortifications can be seen as an outcome of the diffusion of the Capetian military architecture model, transplanted from northern France plains and adapted to the mountainous landscape of south-west France. However, this transfer of technical and architectural knowledge (adaptation) cannot be seen as an important interchange of human values, as it did not involve any idea or concept imported from another culture. ICOMOS also considers that the nominated serial property cannot be seen itself as a milestone in terms of wider diffusion of the Capetian military architecture to other areas. The nominated fortifications may have been part of an indirect process of cultural diffusion but there is no evidence of their direct influence elsewhere, as confirmed by the State Party through the additional information of October 2025. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated.
criteria_en.iv: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated serial property is an exceptional ly homogenous and well -preserved example of a centrally commanded territorial and border military system, which marked the rapid expansion of the kingdom of France towards the Mediterranean world and the Iberian kingdoms. Illustrative of the strengthening and considerable expansion of Capetian power, it is emblematic of the movement towards assertion of sovereign powers that marked th-century Europe. ICOMOS considers that the nominated serial property forms a defensive ensemble with shared architectural language and military functions. The only coherent fortified syst em built by the Capetian kings, it is an exceptional example of the highly standardised Capetian architecture, which historically was part of the rise of the Capetian Crown and its way to control and secure the territory it dominated. It marked a pivotal historic moment in medieval Europe, namely the political shift from feudal fragmentation to the emergence of strong centralised monarchies. The spatial organisation of the nominated fortresses reveals a unified political purpose and a centrally conceived defensive strategy, while their material fabric and architectural language display the vocabulary of Capetian military design carefully adapted to diverse and often demanding topographies. ICOMOS further considers that while the nominated fortresses were scenes of key historical events related to the conquest of Languedoc, they cannot translate to a full representation of this process. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is demonstrated.

criteria.ii: ICOMOS认为标准 ii 未得到证明。
criteria.iv: ICOMOS认为标准 iv 已得到证明。

recommendation_criteria_en.iv: A centrally commanded territorial defensive ensemble with shared architectural language and military functions, The System of Fortresses of the Seneschalty of Carcassonne is an exceptional example of the highly standardised Capetian military architecture carefully adapted to the difficult topography of the territory in which it was implanted. As the only coherent fortification system built by the Capetian kings, it testifies to the strengthening and considerable expansion of the Capetian dynasty power in France in the th-14th centuries, marking the political shift from feudal fragmentation to the emergence of a strong centralised monarchy.

entries_en.serial_selection: ICOMOS considers that the nominated serial property meets criterion (iv), in relation to a narrative focused on the architectural aspects of the nominated property. In this case, ICOMOS considers that the serial approach is justified, and that the selection of component parts is adequate.

payload.intro: 引用 ICOMOS 结论部分对价值标准的判断摘要。

criteria_en.iii: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated serial property bears an exceptional testimony to the living cultural tradition of Italian-style condominio theatres. These theatres represent a fundamental transition, moving from the private domain of the aristocracy and religious institutions to civic institutions accessible to a large paying audience. Their unique financing model, the società condominiale or società di palchettisti (box owners’ society), brought together public bodies and private citizens, particularly from the emerging bourgeoisie, in the construction and management of these spaces. The very architecture of these 18 th and 19th century Italian-style theatres, with their private boxes, stalls and galleries, was deliberately designed to reflect and display the social stratification of the time, embodying the principle of “see and be seen”. Besides from being simple performance venues, these theatres operated as multifunctional and autonomous social and cultural centres, veritable indoor public squares with foyers and ridotti. The longevity and continued use of these theatres, which continue to host a wide variety of cultural events today, confirm their status as a living tradition and as pillars of local civic life. ICOMOS considers that the development of condominio Italian-style theatres in the centres of small and mediumsized towns in Italy bears witness to a phenomenon that saw widespread expansion in the 19 th century in Italy and Europe, followed by Latin America. These theatres, which first appeared in Italian provincial cities from the late 18 th century, were one of the few places where groups of representatives of the emerging bourgeoisie could gather, and where public opinion was formed. These theatres, financed via società condominiali involving public bodies and private individuals in the construction and management of these spaces, embody the transition from the private domain of the aristocracy and religious institutions to civic institutions accessible to a wide audience. With a rare density of still-preserved theatres, the regions of Emilia-Romagna, Marche and Umbria, which form part of the former Papal States, have fostered the development of innovative solutions for theatres, particularly for the distribution of tiered boxes around the stalls, reflectin g the participation of different social classes, seated according to their means, and testifying to the new social hierarchy. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is demonstrated on the basis of a reduced nominated series comprising the following component parts: 02, 03, 04, 05, 06, 07, 09, 12, 16, 17.
criteria_en.iv: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the Italian-style theatres, built in the th and th centuries in Central Italy, constitute a representative group of condominio-type theatres. Central Italy made a vital contribution to the development of the auditorium with tiered rows of boxes, an architectural model that enjoyed lasting success in Europe and beyond. The value of the nominated property lies in its ability to illustrate the evolution of the Italian-style theatre through a range of architectural, acoustic and stage design solutions. The main aspects of this evolution are the transformation of the floor plan/shape, the design of ceilings and other acoustic features, the stage machinery and the layout of the forestage. The theatres of the nominated serial property are still operating. Concentrated in the territory of the former Papal Legations, they have managed, despite periods of interruption to their operation, to preserve the features of their construction and their link with the social and urban fabric. In its interim report, ICOMOS sought clarification concerning the shapes and layout of the auditorium in the Italian-style theatres, and how these reflected the specific nature of condominio theatres. The additional information of February 2026 specifies that the condominio system, which is based on collective ownership and shared financing, enabled these structures to become the centre of local public life, and fa cilitated the historical transition from aristocratic patronage to bourgeoisie entrepreneurship, which made it an exportable model across the world. The spatial configuration of the tieredbox auditoriums reflects a desire to balance participatory decision-making and the requirements for visibility and prestige. The architecture of these theatres materialises a system of collective governance that ensured their sustainability and cultural success within their communities. Whilst ICOMOS acknowledges that the condominio Italian-style theatres built in the th and 19th centuries in Central Italy are part of a significant period in Italian history (the emergence of the bourgeoisie and the Unification of Italy), ICOMOS also considers that t heir specific contribution to the dissemination and expansion of what was known as the “ Italian style” throughout Europe and Latin America has not been demonstrated. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated.

criteria.iii: ICOMOS认为标准 iii 已得到证明。
criteria.iv: ICOMOS认为标准 iv 未得到证明。

recommendation_criteria_en.iii: The system of Italian- style condominio theatres of the 18th and 19th centuries in Central Italy bears witness to the rapid development in town centres of a phenomenon that saw widespread expansion in the th century in Italy and Europe, followed by Latin America. These theatres, which first appeared in the provinces of Emilia-Romagna, Marche and Umbria from around the mid-18 th century on, were one of the few places where groups of representatives of the emerging bourgeoisie, patrons of the theatres, could gather, and where public opinion was formed. These theatres, financed via the società condominiali involving public bodies and private citizens in the construction and management of these spaces, embody the transition from an exclusive private domain to civic institutions accessible to a wide audience. The condominio theatres of the regions of Emilia- Romagna, Marche and Umbria, part of the former Papal States, enabled the development of innovative solutions for theatres, reflecting the participation of different social classes and testifying to the new social hierarchy. The longevity of these theatres, which continue to host a wide variety of cultural events today, bears witness to how deeply they are embedded in local community life.

criteria_en.ii: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated property represents a modern defence system centred around innovative military architecture, hybrid urban planning and advanced artillery expertise, creating a model of territorial defence that influenced European military practice. ICOMOS considers that for this criterion to be justified, the nominated series must provide material evidence of military exchanges of knowledge, techniques and models in military architecture, demonstrating the role of the Raia in broader European developments. 247 ICOMOS considers that the fortifications of the Raia could be further considered as part of a wider European exchange of military ideas, linked to the circulation and development of bastioned fortification from the Renaissance through the 16 th and 17 th centuries. This interpretation requires the frontier system to be considered as a whole, including fortifications on both sides of the PortugueseSpanish border, which were developed in a shared strategic context and in direct response to one another. At this stage, t he nomination lacks concrete documentation of transnational exchanges or adoption of Raia solutions elsewhere, and the comparative analysis with other European and global fortifications is insufficient to demonstrate an important interchange. Moreover, the nominated series does not convincingly demonstrate how innovations developed over time within the Raia were disseminated or influenced military practice beyond the Iberian Peninsula. Justification of this criterion will require a more comprehensive approach, including the chronology of construction, understanding of the movement of military engineers, technological transfers and local adaptations, and deepened comparison with European frontier systems, as well as how and to what extent these interchanges are reflected in relevant fortresses and were important for the development of military architecture. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated.
criteria_en.iv: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated property represents significant examples of 17th and 18th century bulwarked constructions combining advanced design, scientific innovation and coordinated strategy to form an effective defence system, contributing to technical and practical developments of European military architecture and technology. ICOMOS considers that, while the component parts represent notable examples of bulwarked fortifications from this period, their outstanding character cannot be fully demonstrated. The limited selection of only three fortresses from a larger system of 143 fortifications does not adequately reflect the full range of typologies, design innovations, and strategic approaches characteristic of the Raia. Furthermore, the nomination dossier lacks sufficient comparative analysis with other contemporary European or global fortifications to establish the outstanding significance of the nominated property. As a result, there is no clear evidence that the nominated component parts, taken together, illustrate a stage of human history at an outstanding level. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated.
criteria_en.vi: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated property represents a heritage of urban planning integrating military and civilian functions with associated intangible cultural practices, reflecting the role of national identity in shaping the frontier, and forming part of the process that marked the independence of Portugal within the Iberian context. Criterion (vi) requires that a property be directly or tangibly associated with events, living traditions, ideas, beliefs, or artistic and literary works of o utstanding universal significance. In this case, the State Party links the nominated property to the historical process of Portuguese independence and to the development of a frontier system integrating military and civilian functions. While these references relate to historically important developments in the Iberian context, ICOMOS considers that the nomination dossier does not sufficiently demonstrate that the associated events or ideas possess outstanding universal significance. Furthermore, the nature of the association between these processes and the specific component parts included in the nomination remains insufficiently defined, and the attributes expressing this link are not clearly identified. The comparative analysis also does not demonstrate that this association is outstanding in relation to other fortified border systems. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated.

criteria_en.v: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that Ribeira Sacra is an exceptional testimony to a living cultural waterscape that evolved over 1,500 years, with a large number of water works and evidence of cultural knowledge about water management. The State Party considers that the complex settlement patterns of the nominated property demonstrate adapt ation to difficult environmental conditions, including agricultural practices on rugged terrain using socalcos (terraces). The State Party also considers that the different hydraulic technologies for generating energy are significant, including traditional water mills and the 19 th and 20 thcentury hydroelectricity dams and power stations found within the nominated property. T he State Party also considers the interdependence between culture and nature to be central to the proposed Outstanding Universal Value and point s to the intangible cultural heritage traditions of the waterscape. ICOMOS notes the long history of interaction between people and nature in this area, its scenic qualities, and the ways in which the remote and steep terrain and watercourses have been adapted to support human settlements and lifeways. A very wide variety of landscape elements representing multiple historical timeframes have been used to argue for the proposed Outstanding Universal Value. However, ICOMOS does not consider that Ribeira Sacra has been established as an “outstanding example” of a traditional settlement and/or land use. While interesting imprints of relict historical periods are readable in the landscape and continue to be used (such as the pattern of microplots and terracing), the integration and inter -relationships of these with the many other proposed attributes have not been demonstrated. Despite the efforts of the State Party to clarify the basis of the proposed Outstanding Universal Value, ICOMOS considers that this has not portrayed a coherent pattern of settlement/land use that could be considered to be outstanding. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated. 258 ICOMOS considers that criterion (v) has not been demonstrated.

criteria_en.iii: 267 This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated property testifies to the cultural traditions of Roman and Eastern Roman military life, including garrison organisation, water management, religious practices associated with Christianity and the Mithraic cult, and the long-term occupation of a strategic ridge in Southeastern Anatolia. The State Party further presents the fortified complex as an exceptional testimony to a cultural tradition that brings together military and religious practices, stressing that the castrum included not only military and residential structures but also religious buildings, and that an underground rock -cut space provides evidence of Mithraic beliefs and rituals within this military setting. The ensemble of fortifications, internal buildings, hydraulic installations, necropolises and underground rock-cut spaces is presented as reflecting these traditions in a tangible form. ICOMOS considers that, on the basis of the information provided, the testimony of the nominated property to these cultural traditions has not been sufficientl y substantiated in terms of its distinctiveness and evidentiary basis when compared with other known sites of similar type. In particular, the nomination dossier does not clearly demonstrate the distinctiveness of this testimony, nor does it adequately establish its evidentiary basis in comparison with other known sites associated with Roman and Eastern Roman military and settlement systems. ICOMOS further notes that the attributes cited in support of this criterion, namely fortifications, internal buildings, hydraulic installations, necropolises and underground rock-cut spaces, are characteristic elements of Roman and Eastern Roman military and settlement sites more broadly. While their presence at the nominated property is acknowledged, the nomination dossier does not provide sufficient evidence to demonstrate that their configuration, state of preservation or documented use conveys a testimony that is unique or exceptional when assessed against other sites of the same cultural and functional categories considered in the comparative analysis. In particular, the evidentiary link between the physical remains and the specific cultural traditions invoked under this criterion is not demonstrated as being a unique or exceptional testimony in a comparative context. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated.
criteria_en.iv: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the fortified complex, with its defensive circuit, internal architectural layout, hydraulic system, necropolises and associated landscape features, constitutes an example of a Roman and Eastern Roman military camp, a castrum, and its supporting systems, illustrating a significant stage in the history of frontier defence and settlement in the region. In this regard, the State Party emphasises the ridgetop setting of the settlement, the organic layout shaped by topography, the 1.2-kilometre-long fortification wall with bastions and towers, the arrangement of barracks and circulation routes, the administrative complex near the main gate, the Grand Church and underground church, and the extensive hydraulic system including numerous cisterns and a main water channel, as together demonstrating the architectural planning and support systems of a Roman and Eastern Roman garrison. ICOMOS considers that the justification for this criterion depends on a clear demonstration of how the fortifications, internal structures, hydraulic installations and associated features collectively represent, in an outstanding and coherent manner, a specific architectural or technological type illustrating a significant stage in human history. On the basis of the information provided, including the current state of excavation and documentation, this has not been adequately demonstrated, as the nomination dossier does not sufficiently establish the degree to which the architectural layout, construction techniques and technological systems presented exceed, or are meaningfully distinguished from, the ones characteristic of comparable Roman and Eastern Roman military ensembles. In particular, the functional elements cited are not shown to form an integrated and representative ensemble that can be clearly identified as outstanding within the broader typological and regional context. ICOMOS further notes that a full understanding of the architectural and technological ensemble remains constrained by the partial exposure of the built fabric and the uneven level of documentation across different attributes of the nominated property. As a result, the relationship between the fortifications, internal structures, hydraulic installations and associated features is not sufficiently demonstrated to support their interpretation as a clearly defined and exemplary type of an Eastern Roman garrison. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated.
criteria_en.vi: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the underground rock -cut space referred to as a Mithraeum is directly associated with the Mithraic cult and, together with other religious structures at the site, reflects beliefs and rituals of particular significance in the context of the Roman eastern frontier. ICOMOS notes that the identification of this underground space as a Mithraeum, and the strength of its association with the Mithraic cult, are central to the proposed justification under this criterion. In November 2025, the State Party provided further explanations of how Mithraism is evidenced and 268 documented at Zerzevan, describing the underground space as having features presented as typical of Mithraea (including a rectangular plan arranged for seating, niches on the eastern wall including a principal niche presented as being associated with the tauroctony scene, a central altar base, and water-related features presented as being linked to purification rites), and situating this interpretation within a regional comparative context along the Roman eastern frontier. ICOMOS notes, however, that the chronological interpretation advanced in relation to the closure and filling-in of this space remains methodologically uncertain, since the reported discovery of a coin within the fill provides, in stratigraphic terms, a terminus post quem for the deposit rather than conclusive evidence for the date of closure of the chamber itself. In its interim report, ICOMOS further noted that the current interpretation of the underground space relies primarily on functional attribution, while the documentation provided does not conclusively establish the presence of diagnostic elements or contextual evidence necessary to support a direct and tangible association with Mithraic beliefs and ritual practices. In the absence of such evidence, the proposed association remains interpretative rather than demonstrative. In this regard, ICOMOS considers that the architectural configuration of the chamber, as currently exposed, does not exhibit the spatial organisation that is characteristically associated with confirmed Mithraic sanctuaries, and that key typological indicators (including flanking benches, axially positioned cult niches, structured entrance sequences, or tauroctony relief panels) are not presently evidenced. ICOMOS also notes that the excavation information cited by the State Party during the nomination evaluation process indicates that the fill of the chamber was described as mixed and without stratified archaeological findings, which further limits the extent to which the sequence of use, closure and later reuse of the space can presently be established with confidence. ICOMOS further observes that certain carved features cited in the nomination dossier require reconsideration, including a “ray crown” motif above the entrance which, through stylistic analysis, could be identified as a Byzantine cross dating to the 6 th to 8th centuries CE, and that inscriptions previously described as “undecipherable” could be recognised as Syriac and Arabic, indicating a possible medieval activity rather than evidence of Late Antique Mithraic practice. ICOMOS further notes that the inscription may be composite and diachronic rather than a homogeneous text of a single period, reinforcing the need for further specialist epigraphic assessment before firm chronological conclusions are drawn from it. ICOMOS also notes that some features interpreted as ritual fixtures could be interpreted differently as constructional or lighting-related, and that the absence of stratified deposits or securely associated artefacts limits the capacity to establish a specific ritual attribution or chronology for primary use. In February 2026, the State Party reiterated the arguments presented in the nomination dossier supporting its interpretation of the underground rock -cut chamber as a Mithraeum, with an additional provisional decipherment of the debated inscriptions at the entrance of the chamber. Nonetheless, ICOMOS considers that the evidentiary basis for this identification as a Mithraeum, as presented in the nomination dossier and the additional information submitted by the State Party in October 2025 and February 2026, is not sufficiently demonstrated, particularly in relation to architectural form, associated features and material culture. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated. ICOMOS considers that since the criteria for justifying the potential Outstanding Universal Value have not been demonstrated, respective supporting attributes cannot be confirmed.

criteria_en.ii: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the six Berlin housing estates already inscribed on the World Heritage List, together with the nominated extension, testify to a broad housing reform movement that made a decisive contribution to improving housing and living conditions in Berlin. Their quality of urban, architectural and garden design, as well as the housing standards developed during this period, served as guidelines for social housing constructed since then, both in and outside Germany. The nominated extension, being one of the most representative estates of the comprehensive Berlin housing reform, enhances the justification of this criterion through its innovative urban planning solution which integrates the estate into a heavily wooded suburban landscape, and through the combination of architecture, modern design, landscaping and the contemporary transport ation infrastructure. ICOMOS considers that the nominated extension contributes to expanding and amplifying the justification of this criterion.
criteria_en.iv: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the six Berlin housing estates already inscribed on the World Heritage List, together with the nominated extension, exemplify new urban and architectural typologies, designed in the search for improved social living conditions. Fresh design solutions, as well as technical and aesthetic innovations, were incorporated by the leading modernist architects who contributed to their construction. T he nominated extension complements the World Heritage series by providing an outstanding example of a mixed urban typology of garden cities combined with large housing estates through the integration of the surrounding natural environment into the urban plan. The technical innovations such as the prefabricated building elements, standardised house types, aesthetic innovations of modern and extensive colourful design solutions, and the infrastructural concept of the underground station combining the transport infrastructure with a social and cultural centre, contribute to the development of the serial typological urban and architectural ensemble of the Berlin Modernism Housing Estates and its significance in the context of early 20 th-century modernist architecture. ICOMOS considers that the nominated extension contributes to expanding and amplifying the justification of this criterion. ICOMOS concurs with the identified attributes proposed by the State Party for the nominated extension, and considers that they complement those of the World Heritage series. ICOMOS considers that the nominated extension of Waldsiedlung Zehlendorf meets criteria (ii) and (iv), and contributes to expanding and amplifying the alreadyapproved justification of these criteria.

recommendation_criteria_en.ii: The seven Berlin housing estates provide an outstanding expression of a broad housing reform movement that made a decisive contribution to improving housing and living conditions in Berlin. Their quality of urban, architectural and garden design, as well as the housing standards developed during the period, served as guidelines for social housing constructed since then, both in and outside Germany.
recommendation_criteria_en.iv: The seven Berlin housing estates are exceptional examples of new urban and architectural typologies, designed in the search for improved social living conditions. Fresh design solutions and technical and aesthetic innovations were incorporated by the leading modernist architects who participated in their design and construction.

criteria_en.ii: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the nominated serial property bears witness to the fusion between European artistic and technological influences in the late 19 th century and the cultural and territorial characteristics of the Amazonian landscape. This synthesis is embodied in the design, form and materials of the buildings and their adjoining public spaces. The theatres served as catalysts for a dynamic exchange between international performances and regional artistic traditions. ICOMOS considers that both component parts of the nominated serial property, built in the late 19 th century, have a chronological link with the rubber boom in the Amazon (1879-1912). ICOMOS notes that despite the additional information of October 2025, highlighting a similar geographical situation, the use of local materials in their construction and their integration into international musical theatre circuits, the notion of an exchange of influences has not been justified. In effect, the existence of one or more places where ideas, technologies and artistic practices intersected and led to innovative and unprecedented forms is not demonstrated here. Artists were commissioned by the authorities of Belém or Manaus to produce monumental works of art; artworks produced in Europe were commissioned by both cities; urban planning and landscaping projects were undertaken for the creation of the city of Manaus and the expansion of Belém, in the spirit of Haussmann’s Paris; and a European workforce was recruited and worked alongside Brazilian artists and artisans to create painted and sculpted works evocative of the vegetation and landscape of the Amazon. However, ICOMOS considers that these imports of art objects, concepts and know -how demonstrate an essentially one-way adoption and reflect a reference to a European lifestyle and culture, to the "Belle Époque", rather than a substantial exchange of influences. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is not demonstrated.
criteria_en.iv: This criterion is justified by the State Party on the grounds that the exceptional nature of the nominated theatres, as examples of architecture and urban planning, illustrates the profound social, economic and cultural transformations that followed the Industrial Revolution. This achievement can be seen in their architectural eclecticism, which combines industrial materials, such as iron structures, with local resources, such as stone and Amazonian woods. ICOMOS considers that the construction of a theatre and the development of a public space within a ci ty are common phenomena that are not exceptional in themselves, nor is the creation of cultural and leisure facilities in city centres, associated with their expansion. The construction of Italian-style theatres in the th century was a phenomenon that occurred throughout Europe and the Americas, particularly Latin America. The financing of these cultural facilities was then facilitated by the growing wealth of countries participating in the Industrial Revolution, the resulting national cultural policies and the rise of the bourgeoisie, who sought to display their prosperity through these prestigious projects. The nominated serial property participates in this general trend, without exhibiting any exceptional or prototypical characteristics. However, ICOMOS considers that the Teatro Amazonas (component part 002) and the Largo de São Sebastião represent the culmination of the construction of a modern capital in the heart of the Amazon rainforest. The city was built thanks to the rubber boom, a global phenom enon associated with the Industrial Revolution. The Teatro Amazonas was built by European architects, engineers and artists, with contributions from Brazilian artists, using technologies and materials imported from Europe combined with local resources. A true architectural showcase, it reflects the immense wealth generated by rubber exploitation at the end of the th century, as well as the ideals of progress, civilisation and European cosmopolitanism that the local elite wished to promote in the heart of the Amazon. ICOMOS considers that the situation is different for Belém, whose urban history covers a longer timeframe, from the 17th to the 20th century, and is not solely linked to the rubber boom. ICOMOS considers that this criterion is demonstrated by the Teatro Amazonas (component part 002) and the Largo de São Sebastião in Manaus alone.

recommendation_criteria_en.iv: The Teatro Amazonas and the Largo de São Sebastião symbolise the construction of a modern capital in the heart of the Amazon rainforest, made possible by the exploitation of rubber, a phenomenon associated with the Industrial Revolution. Built by European architects and artists, with contributions from Brazilian artists, and combining technologies and materials imported from Europe with local resources, the Teatro Amazonas is a luxurious Italian- style theatre, an architectural showcase reflecting the wealth generated by rubber production, as well as the ideals of progress, civilisation and European cosmopolitanism that the local elite wished to promote in the Amazon. 297

criteria_en.vi:
This criterion is justified by the State Party on two grounds. First, the nominated property is presented as the birthplace of and a global reference point for the mythological traditions associated with the Twelve Gods of the ancient Greek religion, while the remains of the temples and sanctuaries at Dion on the lower slopes of the mountain are the most remarkable expression of the cult of the Olympian Gods and its longevity over a millennium. 

Second, the State Party considers that the exceptional geomorphological and climatic elements have been mythologised throughout the ages to the present day, as reflected in modern toponyms such as the “Pantheon” (Mytikas peak), the “Throne of Zeus” (Stefani peak), and the “Plateau of the Muses”. The legacy of the Twel ve Gods and their dwelling on Mount Olympus have inspired literature, art, popular culture, and science throughout the world and across the centuries to the present day. Furthermore, the mountain is still used as a metaphor for divine order and human aspiration. According to the State Party, Mount Olympus stands as a symbolic topos (name and concept) of the myth, grandeur and excellence, with people bestowing its name on many places, massifs, towns and villages around the globe and even beyond to the planet Mars.

ICOMOS considers that Mount Olympus is an outstanding example of a mountain landscape with particular geological characteristics that have inspired, and can clearly be linked to, an ancient religious or mythological expression, which in turn has maintained a centuries-long relevance to the present day and has had an impact far beyond its region of origin. The works of Homer (Iliad and Odyssey) and Hesiod (Theogony) in particular captured the idea of Mount Olympus as the abode of the Twelve Greek Gods, and the dissemination of their literature around the world has given Mount Olympus far -reaching relevance. The existence and importance of the link between the myth and the mountain (component part 1) is strengthened by the presence of the archaeological remains of the ancient city of Dion (component part 2). This archaeological site is closely associated with the cult of Zeus, the ruler of the Twelve Gods, and from where the forbidding mountain peaks can clearly be observed. Further traces of the worship of the gods on Mount Olympus can be found, for example, on St. Antonios peak, where evidence of an open-air sanctuary dedicated to the cult of Zeus has been discovered. However, ICOMOS considers that the documentation of the attributes that support the proposed Outstanding Universal Value is not sufficient at the present stage. The physical aspects of the nominated serial property, including the awe-inspiring mountain landscape and the archaeological remains of the service to the Olympian Gods, need more detailed documentation. The relevance and far -reaching impact of the myth also have to be documented in a more detailed and systematic manner, including, for example, more research on the history of the diffusion of the myth and the relevance it has today. ICOMOS considers that this criterion will be fully justified once the attributes of the proposed Outstanding Universal Value are fully documented. ICOMOS considers that criterion (vi) will be fully justified once the attributes of the proposed Outstanding Universal Value are fully documented.

ICOMOS considers that criterion (vi) will be fully justified once the attributes of the proposed Outstanding Universal Value are fully documented.

recommendation_criteria_en.vi: ICOMOS considers that criterion (vi) will be fully justified once the attributes of the proposed Outstanding Universal Value are fully documented.

payload.brief_zh: ICOMOS推荐OUV声明中的Brief synthesis原文已抽取；中文页面可在此基础上进行工作译文和审校。
payload.brief_en:
The serial property comprises the best-preserved parts of six historic cities located on the islands of Ngazidja and Ndzuwani in the Comoros. They developed from urban nuclei that emerged around at least the 11th century and evolved into city-states around the 14th-15th century, becoming eventually the principal urban centres of the sultanates established in the archipelago of Comoros within the framework of the formation of a Muslim elite linked to the Swahili world. This heritage ensemble, encompassing urban, architectural, and intangible elements, reflects the richness and specificity of Swahili cultural tradition within the Comorian island context. The former sultanic medinas have preserved their old urban morphology and fabric, and an exceptional set of traditions and social practices still alive today. 

Compact fabrics, with centrally organised palaces and public squares (bangwe, mpangahari), stone houses and defensive structures, the medinas showcase insular urban forms and architectural expressions that resulted from cultural exchanges related to migration and Indian Ocean trade networks, while displaying a spatial logic dictated by a hierarchical system of social organisation based on matrilineal and matrilocal customs, age-group systems, and patrilineal Islamic traditions. The Comorian historic cities, though founded upon the same fundamental cultural and social basis, evolved along distinct trajectories shaped by their geographic, political, social and historical contexts.

payload.summary_en: Based on the nomination dossier, and according to the State Party, the key attributes of the proposed Outstanding Universal Value are: the urban planning and urban morphology; urban fabric; architectural forms and public and ceremonial spaces of social interaction; decorative elements of the urban fabric (carved doors, decorated beams, zidaka-niches, coral and volcanic stone masonry); as well as the associated intangible heritage in the form of cultural practices, traditions, social systems, celebrations, vernacular architectural techniques based on the use of local materials.
payload.summary: ICOMOS/申报文本在价值论证部分集中列明支撑OUV的主要属性；本条保留原文，后续可在页面中继续细分属性组。

0.group: the urban planning and urban morphology

2.group: architectural forms and public and ceremonial spaces of social interaction

3.group: decorative elements of the urban fabric (carved doors, decorated beams, zidaka-niches, coral and volcanic stone masonry)

4.group: as well as the associated intangible heritage in the form of cultural practices, traditions, social systems, celebrations, vernacular architectural techniques based on the use of local materials.

5.group: ICOMOS notes that the proposed justification is centred on the period of sultanates in the Comoros, between the 14th- 15th and 19th centuries, as reflected by the principal urban centres of two islands of the archipelago. The specific historic period, however, cannot be considered in itself to carry an Outstanding Universal Value. Moreover, the Comorian medinas are more than a result of the establishment of the sultanates. They started developing much earlier, under the system of territorial chiefdoms. In the 14th-15th century, they had not yet reached their full urban morphology. The period of their greatest development falls apparently in the 17th-18th century only the city walls erected at that time marking the extent the medinas. The historic trajectory of the Comorian medinas is therefore best understood on a longer-term historical timescale, which allows an appreciation of how the historic cities emerged from nuclei of clan-based communities to evolve into fully urbanised and fortified centres, embodying the synthesis of local continuity and external exchanges. ICOMOS concurs with the reasons for which the State Party considers the year 1886 and the start of the colonial period as the end of the sultanate era rather than the formal abolition of sultanates. ICOMOS considers that the nominated serial property is more than a reflection of the existence of the political power of the sultans in the Comoros

payload.summary_en: Despite gaps in the comparative analysis, ICOMOS considers that the nominated serial property can be considered exceptional among its comparators. ICOMOS considers that, despite some omissions, the comparative analysis justifies consideration of this property for the World Heritage List.
payload.summary: 比较分析章节已抽取原文和主要结论；比较对象采用正则初步识别，需在页面中人工复核名录状态和比较意义。

comparators.2: At the global level, Historic Jeddah, the Gate to Makkah (Saudi Arabia, 2014, criteria (ii)

entries_en.integrity: The integrity of the nominated serial property is based on the inclusion of a sufficient area of each historic town to understand their urban morphology, and key elements of the urban fabric, including public and private spaces whose meaning is linked to the traditional social organisation and which host cultural practices. Associations between the built environment and its natural setting are functionally important to support the protection of the nominated property. As a serial nomination, integrity is also a measure of whether the component parts individually and collectively contain all the attributes necessary to express in a substantial way the proposed Outstanding Universal Value of the nominated property as a whole, and how each component part contributes to that value. Integrity is furthermore a measure of the intactness of the attributes. Only the best-preserved parts of each medina have been included within the nominated serial property, with the 22 remaining intra-muros areas and some immediate setting included within the buffer zones. ICOMOS considers that this approach is in principle adequate, as it takes into consideration the state of conservation of the historic cities, leaving out elements that have lost their integrity.
entries_en.authenticity: The authenticity of the nominated serial property is based on the form and design, materials, and function, as well as location and setting of the historical vestiges of the selected medinas, and the associated intangible heritage. Authenticity depends on the degree of credibility and truthfulness of information sources on the value of the nominated property. These sources of information must be considered and judged primarily within the cultural contexts to which they belong. The urban form, spatial organisation, and the "spirit of place" remain strong in all six medinas, and the authenticity of their use and function is also very high. However, material authenticity is more heterogeneous, particularly where concrete reconstructions have been carried out without clear conservation principles. The relationship with the sea is not fully preserved today due to modern developments.
entries_en.boundaries: An estimated 20,000 inhabitants (2024) live in six component parts, with the same number of residents projected in the buffer zones. The delimitation of boundaries follows a common logic. The component parts comprise compact areas, centred on the intra-muros part of each medina, encompassing only those sections of each historic city where integrity and legibility remain the strongest, while maintaining a sufficiently large buffer zone to act as a visual, functional, and regulatory filter. Depending on the medina, from thirty to seventy percent of intra-muros areas have been included within each component part. The boundaries are designed to follow existing routes.

entries.integrity: 依据Integrity章节是否明确认为条件已满足/已证明初判。
entries.authenticity: 依据Authenticity章节是否明确认为条件已满足/已证明初判。
entries.boundaries: 依据Boundaries章节对遗产区/缓冲区边界是否充分、是否需修订的表述初判。

entries.protection_property: Legal framework exists but is not yet fully implementable; decrees, registration/listing, and enhanced national protection remain unfinished.
entries.protection_buffer_zone: Buffer-zone legal measures and urban regulations are projected rather than operational; added protection depends on future legal measures.
entries.conservation: State of conservation is very fragile; no programmed conservation activities are underway; ICOMOS calls for urgent structured conservation planning.
entries.management: Management is fragmented; roles, local authority involvement, formal/traditional integration and HIA procedures are not sufficiently clear.
entries.threats_addressed: ICOMOS states factors are not effectively managed because of lack of capacity, resources and an operational legal framework; no risk management plan exists yet.

entries_en.conservation:
ICOMOS considers that inventories that should constitute the baseline documentation for planning purposes are in place. However, a state of conservation assessment is urgently needed to develop and implement a conservation programme for the nominated serial property and individual conservation plans for each component part.
ICOMOS considers that it would be advisable that the monitoring system is further developed and is conceived for easy integration of its outcomes into the Periodic Reporting questionnaire. The monitoring system should encompass all the attributes of the proposed Outstanding Universal Value, including the intangible aspects.
entries_en.management: In summary, ICOMOS considers that the legal protection of the nominated serial property needs to be completed to provide a solid, legitimate, and binding basis for the management, conservation, monitoring, and coordination activities. An integrated and collaborative management system at the level of the nominated serial property as a whole must engage appropriately State, regional and local authorities, alongside the local communities. The existing traditional management system should be integrated with the formal administrative mechanisms. The management plan should be further implemented. Planning of tourism should be aligned with other state strategies.

a.source_en: a) Continuing the efforts to finalise and adopt the necessary legislative and regulatory texts to provide a solid and enhanced legal protection to the property,
a.summary: ICOMOS正式建议条目；已按同事版主题体系完成逐条分类。

c.summary: ICOMOS正式建议条目；已按同事版主题体系完成逐条分类。

d.source_en: d) Conducting, in priority, a state of conservation assessment and, based on it, developing an integrated conservation programme for the serial property as well as individual conservation plans for each component part,
d.summary: ICOMOS正式建议条目；已按同事版主题体系完成逐条分类。

e.source_en: e) Developing further the management system in which state, regional and local authorities must be appropriately engaged, and integrating the traditional management system with the formal administrative mechanisms,
e.summary: ICOMOS正式建议条目；已按同事版主题体系完成逐条分类。
