mentions.source_en: The integrity of a nominated property is a measure of the completeness or intactness of the attributes that convey its proposed Outstanding Universal Value. For a serial nomination, integrity is also a measure of whether the component parts individually and collectively contain all the attributes necessary to express in a substantial way the proposed Outstanding Universal Value of the nominated property as a whole and how each component part contributes to that value. The integrity of the nominated serial property is linked to the mountain (component part 1), particularly its aweinspiring, cloud-shrouded steepness and inaccessibility, as well as to the archaeological city of Dion in the foothills of Mount Olympus (component part 2), and the myth of the mountain as the abode of the Twelve Greek Gods that has become a cultural reference point all over the world. The physical spaces referred to in the Greek mythology as the abode of the Twelve Olympian Gods are included in the nominated property but are not described in detail by the State Party. The two component parts individually and collectively are believed to contain all the attributes necessary to convey the proposed Outstanding Universal Value. However, no detailed plans of the Archaeological site of Dion, the second component part of the nominated property, have been provided, nor has the archaeological landscape surrounding the site been described in adequate detail.

entries_en.integrity:
ICOMOS therefore considers that the size of the nominated property and the type and quantity of cultural and natural attributes supporting the proposed Outstanding Universal Value are adequate. However, the description and documentation of the completeness and intactness of the two component parts will have to be improved.

ICOMOS considers that the integrity of the nominated series as a whole, as well as the integrity of each of the component parts, have been demonstrated.
entries_en.authenticity: COMOS considers that the authenticity of the nominated series as a whole, as well as the authenticity of each of the component parts, have been demonstrated.
entries_en.integrity_authenticity_conclusion: In conclusion, ICOMOS considers that the conditions of integrity and of authenticity of the whole nominated series and of the individual component parts have been met.
entries_en.boundaries: Only thirty inhabitants live within the boundaries of the nominated property (2021 census). In the buffer zone there are about 10,400 inhabitants living in parts of eight settlements and one monastery (2021 census). The nominated property includes Mount Olympus and its wider area. The boundaries are based directly on the legal and management framework of the Mount Olympus National Park, established under Presidential Decree (Government Gazette 610/D’/2021), encompassing Zones A, B and C (the Nature Protection and National Park zones) while Zone D, the Regional Zone, falls primarily within the buffer zone. They also coincide with the limits of the area designated under the European Union’s Natura 2000 network as both a Special Area of Conservation and a Special Protection Area, named “Mount Olympus – Oros Olympos”.

entries_en.conservation: Documentation of cultural attributes is not clear or systematic enough, and ICOMOS requests better maps, plans, photographs, models and scientific documentation.

entries.protection_property: Protection and management are generally adequate and have proven efficient, but the Special Urban Plan must be completed and approved as a priority.
entries.protection_buffer_zone: ICOMOS asks for revised/adjusted boundaries around Litochoro and operationalisation of the Special Urban Plan, indicating buffer/boundary protection is not yet sufficiently resolved.
entries.conservation: Documentation of cultural attributes is not clear or systematic enough, and ICOMOS requests better maps, plans, photographs, models and scientific documentation.
entries.management: The planned Coordinating Committee must be operationalised and management must better balance natural and cultural heritage, with stronger local participation.
entries.threats_addressed: Tourism, grazing, logging, hunting, flooding and other factors are identified, but integrated tourism strategy, carrying capacity and visitor monitoring are still required.

payload.brief_en: Located on the Baltic Sea coast, Gdynia Modernist City Centre embraces the two most significant east-west and north-south streets, featuring interwar planning blocks and the majority of the city centre's preserved buildings from that period, which illustrate the large-scale application of modernist ideas in architectural design.

payload.summary_en: Three key attributes were identified: a modern coherent urban structure; a city centre designed in close connection with the sea, port and landscape; and a large, functionally diverse and technically modern architectural complex.

entries_en.integrity: ICOMOS considers that the conditions of integrity are yet to be fully met.
entries_en.authenticity: ICOMOS considers that the authenticity of the nominated property has been demonstrated.
entries_en.boundaries: The nominated property boundaries still do not include all the evidence and supporting attributes of the modernist planning principles; the expanded buffer zone is welcomed and appears adequate.

entries_en.protection_property: The protection of the nominated property appears adequate.
entries_en.protection_buffer_zone: The legal provisions applicable to the buffer zone have been clarified and appear adequate; ICOMOS suggests verifying their long-term effectiveness.
entries_en.conservation: ICOMOS considers that the documentation, conservation measures, and monitoring system for the nominated property are adequate.
entries_en.management: ICOMOS considers that the management of the nominated property is satisfactory and is consistently implemented in a participatory manner.
entries_en.threats_addressed: The property faces development pressures and management challenges, including incompatible scale, courtyard outbuildings, and boundary exclusions.

payload.brief_en: Sebastia, located near Nablus in the West Bank, in Palestine, preserves the archaeological record of the successive occupation of the site by different civilisations from at least the 9th century BCE through the Hellenistic, Roman, and Byzantine periods, with later architectural remains dating to the Crusader era, as well as the Ayyubid/Mamluk and Ottoman periods.

entries_en.integrity: In the absence of a technical evaluation mission, ICOMOS has been unable to fully evaluate integrity; land expropriation plans make it highly vulnerable.
entries_en.authenticity: In the absence of a technical evaluation mission, ICOMOS has been unable to evaluate fully the authenticity of the nominated property.
entries_en.boundaries: The nominated property boundary is logical, but the basis and suitability of the buffer zone could not be fully evaluated.

entries_en.protection_property: Adequate legal frameworks formally exist, but territorial division and restricted access obstruct effective implementation.
entries_en.protection_buffer_zone: Planning and regulatory frameworks exist, but their effectiveness and implementation could not be fully assessed and are constrained on the ground.
entries_en.conservation: Conservation initiatives are hindered by governance challenges; a holistic approach is currently impossible to implement and monitoring requires strengthening.
entries_en.management: The separation of administrative authority and the ongoing conflict hinder holistic protection and management and make the system not fully operational.
entries_en.threats_addressed: ICOMOS considers that documented, imminent expropriation and land-seizure measures constitute an emergency situation and threaten the integrity of the property.

RM.source_en: ICOMOS recommends that a Reactive Monitoring mission be invited to the property, if this is feasible, to establish a Desired state of conservation and a programme of corrective measures to remove the property from the List of World Heritage in Danger.

evidence.quote_en: The integrity of the nominated serial property is based on the inclusion of a sufficient area of each historic town to understand their urban morphology, and key elements of the urban fabric, including public and private spaces whose meaning is linked to the traditional social organisation and which host cultural practices. Associations between the built environment and its natural setting are functionally important to support the protection of the nominated property. As a serial nomination, integrity is also a measure of whether the component parts individually and collectively contain all the attributes necessary to express in a substantial way the proposed Outstanding Universal Value of the nominated property as a whole, and how each component part contributes to that value. Integrity is furthermore a measure of the intactness of the attributes.

An estimated 20,000 inhabitants (2024) live in six component parts, with the same number of residents projected in the buffer zones. The delimitation of boundaries follows a common logic. The component parts comprise compact areas, centred on the intra-muros part of each medina, encompassing only those sections of each historic city where integrity and legibility remain the strongest, while maintaining a sufficiently large buffer zone to act as a visual, functional, and regulatory filter. Depending on the medina, from thirty to seventy percent of intra-muros areas have been included within each component part. The boundaries are designed to follow existing routes.

evidence.quote_en: According to the nomination dossier, the nominated series includes all the necessary attributes to convey the proposed Outstanding Universal Value. Most architectural elements within each component part have survived, and their historic layout, spatial logic, and reciprocal relationships remain legible because little development has obliterated them. Changes to the immediate and wider setting have been limited and contribute to the understanding of the historic and cultural significance of the nominated serial property. Functional structures and their machinery have been retained at some roças, allowing an appreciation of how the agro-industrial system functioned.

through over-renovation and the erasure of historical surfaces. Following an assessment of threats, the State Party has established preventive measures to protect the property from degradation, in collaboration with local authorities, communities, and roça concession holders. However, their implementation depends on the availability of human and financial resources. ICOMOS considers that the state of conservation is uneven, with structures at Monte Café and Agua Izé (component parts 1 and 2) in critical condition, and that the main factor affecting the nominated property is an inappropriate development, driven by a lack of capacity and resources and by vulnerable socio-economic conditions.

The number of permanent inhabitants varies across the nominated component parts. At Monte Café (component part 1), there are 1,100 inhabitants in the nominated component part and an additional 400 inhabitants in the buffer zone. At Agua Izé (component part 2), 2,300 inhabitants are numbered and an additional 700 people live in the buffer zone. At Diogo Vaz (component part 3), 600 inhabitants live in the component part. At Sao Joao (component part 4), only five people live in the component part.

evidence.quote_en: of the nominated property, but most importantly to the residents of the village. The recent atmospheric events have clearly highlighted the vulnerability of the site and gaps in the disaster risk preparedness of the State Party to respond effectively to natural disasters, in order to avoid negative outcomes, or reduce their impact. Accordingly, ICOMOS considers that the implementation of the natural disaster risk management plan for the nominated property is an urgent necessity and should be prioritised by the State Party. It should be accompanied by an emergency plan that needs to be developed taking into consideration the specificities of the terrain and the risks that it poses to residents and visitors.

Integrity The integrity of the nominated property is based on the wholeness and intactness of the urban morphology and the fabric of Sidi Bou Saïd, as well as the religious practices. The nominated property includes within its

There are approximately 1,500 inhabitants in the nominated property (2023 census), and 6,500 in the buffer zone. The boundaries include the historic centre of the village together with a large part of the promontory. It largely follows the boundary of the historical zone of Sidi Bou Saïd designated by the 1915 Beylical decree but skirts the later buildings which break with its traditional architectural style on the north-western edges of the historical zone and excludes the marina. In response to the recommendations of ICOMOS included in the interim report, the State Party adjusted the boundaries of the nominated property to the south, by including the El Marassi hotel and a residential complex adjacent to Korsi Essoleh, both of which were built in the aesthetic resembling the structures of the nominated property. The immediate surroundings of the village have been included in the buffer zone to ensure control of the developments near the nominated property and protect the panoramas.

Recommendations with respect to inscription ICOMOS recommends that the Village of Sidi Bou Saïd: Hub of Cultural and Spiritual Inspiration in the Mediterranean, Tunisia, be inscribed on the World Heritage List on the basis of criterion (iii). Recommended Statement of Outstanding Universal Value Brief synthesis The Village of Sidi Bou Saïd is located on top of a hill overlooking the Mediterranean Sea, in northern Tunisia. It developed as a sanctuary-village as a result of a process of aggregation around the mausoleum of a Sufi saint – Abou Saïd Khalaf Ibn Yahya El Tamimi El Beji, known as Sidi Bou Saïd – supported by successive ruling dynasties, which, from the 16th century onwards, fostered the sacralisation of the place by associating their political legitimacy with the baraka (blessing) of the saint.

There are 44,500 inhabitants in the five nominated component parts and 195,000 inhabitants in the buffer zones. According to the State Party, the boundaries of the component parts are based on the inclusion of the proposed attributes and are delineated using topography, road networks, watercourses, landforms, and building 62 outlines. Each component part has a buffer zone which is delineated according to the industrial function and environmental context. Guidance and controls over future urban and rural development are in place for the buffer zones.

Recommendations with respect to inscription ICOMOS recommends that the Jingdezhen Handicraft Porcelain Industry Sites, China, be inscribed on the World Heritage List on the basis of criteria (ii), (iii), (iv) and (vi). Recommended Statement of Outstanding Universal Value Brief synthesis The Jingdezhen Handicraft Porcelain Industry Sites are located in an area with rich reserves of porcelain stone, kaolin clay, firewood forests and water transportation. Five component parts represent the handicraft porcelain industry system of Jingdezhen from the 10th to the 19th century.

As a serial nomination, integrity is also a measure of whether the component parts individually and collectively contain all the attributes necessary to express in a substantial way the proposed Outstanding Universal Value of the nominated property as a whole, and how each component part contributes to that value. Excavated architectural and archaeological vestiges of the monastic complex of Sarnath are located within the proposed boundaries, constituting two separate archaeological sites, demarcated physically by compound walls and metal railings. ICOMOS notes that the Shri Digambar Jain Temple, which is part of the archaeological park, is not included in component part 02. There are no inhabitants in the nominated property. The buffer zone, with approximately 6,000 inhabitants (2020 estimate), includes the area of the All-India Radio Station, the garden of Spiritual Wisdom, an extensive area between the two component parts where several temples and monasteries of different Buddhist communities have been constructed in modern times and where the Archaeological Museum is located, as well as the Sarnath Mini Zoo, and the Buddha theme park. To the west, the buffer zone includes some low-rise developments.

Recommendations with respect to inscription ICOMOS recommends that the Ancient Buddhist Site, Sarnath, Varanasi, India, be inscribed on the World Heritage List on the basis of criteria (iii) and (vi). Recommended Statement of Outstanding Universal Value Brief synthesis Located within Varanasi city, state of Uttar Pradesh, India, this serial property composed of two component parts (Chaukhandi Stupa, and the Archaeological Remains of Sarnath) comprises all the known and preserved archaeological and architectural vestiges of the ancient site of Sarnath, believed to be the place that Gautama Buddha visited in the 6th century BCE. Defined by the Buddha as one of the four key sites of Buddhist pilgrimage, Sarnath developed over centuries through royal, monastic and lay patronage.
ICOMOS considers that the comparative analysis justifies consideration of this property for the World Heritage List.

According to the additional information provided by the State Party in October 2025, nine villages (Gāzorkhān, Tavān, Zardchāl, Māshā kelāyeh, Garmārūd, Šams Kelāyeh, Qosṭinlār, Āghāgīr and Ilān) are located within the buffer zones of the nominated component parts, with a total population numbering 1,936 inhabitants. The seven nominated component parts and their buffer zones are located within the Alamūt region which serves as a Natural defensive territory. The State Party argues that the most important consideration in defining the boundaries of the nominated property has been to consider the physical remains of each nominated fortification, with surrounding areas and related key features included within the boundaries where necessary. According to the October 2025 additional information, these features include ancient water-supply systems, historical access routes and parts of traditional orchards, and medicinal plant habitat areas. The buffer zones, on the other hand, are usually delimited by ridge lines, or by mountain slopes and non-natural boundaries such as roads.

There are 496 inhabitants in the nominated component parts, and 12,489 inhabitants in the buffer zone. Most of the component parts have no inhabitants, other than the Fujiwara Palace site (component part 013, 440 inhabitants), the Asuka-dera Temple Site (component part 005, 36 inhabitants), and very small numbers at the sites of the Asuka Palace Site, Tachibana-dera Temple Site, Yamada-dera Temple Site, Kawara-dera Temple Site, and the Motoyakushiji Temple Site (component parts 001, 006, 007, 008 and 015). There is some private land within the nominated component parts. Additional information received in February 2026 outlined the processes for the acquisition of private land. The boundaries of the component parts are based on their designations as Historic Site, Special Historic Site, and Place of Scenic Beauty under the Law for the Protection of Cultural Properties or imperial property under the Imperial House Law.

Recommendations with respect to inscription ICOMOS recommends that the Ancient Capitals of Asuka and Fujiwara, Japan, be inscribed on the World Heritage List on the basis of criteria (ii) and (iii). Recommended Statement of Outstanding Universal Value Brief synthesis The Ancient Capitals of Asuka and Fujiwara are a serial property of nineteen component parts that are associated with two consecutive early capitals on the Japanese archipelago established through interchanges with kingdoms in China and the Korean Peninsula. Located in the Nara Basin, these sites attest to the formative processes of the emergence and establishment of 102 centralised state systems in East Asia, modelled on the Chinese ritsuryo system.

ICOMOS considers that there are several additional properties on the World Heritage List and the Tentative Lists of States Parties that express some similarities to the nominated property and therefore could be compared. ICOMOS considers that the comparative analysis justifies consideration of this property for the World Heritage List.

Integrity Integrity is a measure of wholeness, intactness, and absence of threats to the nominated serial property and its attributes. As a serial nomination, integrity is also a measure of whether the component parts individually and collectively contain all the attributes necessary to express in a substantial way the proposed Outstanding Universal Value of the nominated property as a whole, and how each component parts contribute to that value. ICOMOS considers that each of the eight component parts contributes to the nominated series in a substantial way. Together, they represent the regional characteristics of Xiongnu tombs across the administrative and territorial divisions of the Xiongnu empire.

Neither the component parts nor their buffer zones are reported to have any inhabitants. Nevertheless, the management plan mentions the need to implement natural disaster prevention measures, including the relocation of two herder households from the buffer zones at Duurlig Nars (component part 2) and Gol Mod II (component part 4). The initial delimitation of the boundaries of the nominated component parts and buffer zones were corrected and updated by the State Party in November 2025, in response to questions posed by ICOMOS. Revised maps were also submitted at that time. Each component part is delimited with a boundary that accords with national legislation, with a minimum distance of 200 metres from the outermost tombs.

Recommendations with respect to inscription ICOMOS recommends that The Cemetery Complexes of the Xiongnu Nobility, Mongolia, be inscribed on the World Heritage List on the basis of criterion (iii). Recommended Statement of Outstanding Universal Value Brief synthesis This serial property comprises eight funerary complexes of the Xiongnu nobility. The Xiongnu, ancient predecessors of a confederation of Eurasian nomadic peoples, established a powerful empire between the 3rd century BCE and the 2nd century CE that occupied a vast territory centred on what is now the modern state of Mongolia.

Following the receipt of this additional insight, ICOMOS considers that the comparative analysis is adequate. ICOMOS considers that the comparative analysis justifies consideration of this property for the World Heritage.

evidence.quote_en: The integrity of the nominated property is based on the wholeness of the Wat Phra Mahathat monastery complex. This includes all the religious buildings and the sacred sand ground on which they stand, their layout and spatial relationships, the views to and from the complex and the intangible rituals and traditions associated with the complex. The intangible aspects find expression in the associated tangible elements. Integrity is also a measure of the intactness of the attributes, and the way major pressures on these are managed.

Integrity The integrity of the nominated property is based on the wholeness of the Wat Phra Mahathat monastery complex. This includes all the religious buildings and the sacred sand ground on which they stand, their layout and spatial relationships, the views to and from the complex and the intangible rituals and traditions associated with the complex. The intangible aspects find expression in the associated tangible elements. Integrity is also a measure of the intactness of the attributes, and the way major pressures on these are managed.

In 2024, there were fifty-one monastic residents (monks, novices, devotees, and laypeople) in the nominated property, and 31,172 individuals from eighteen communities residing in the buffer zone. The boundaries of the nominated property follow the approximately rectangular shape of the historical outline of the monastery and are legally protected. The buffer zone of the nominated property reflects the Ayutthaya period of the Old Town of Nakhon Si Thammarat. The legal provisions for its protection are dedicated to the entire Old Town, which extends beyond the buffer zone in both the north and south directions. It controls building heights and designs, prohibits construction, modification or change of use for specific types of buildings, and controls the speed and weight limits of vehicles on certain roads.

ICOMOS considers that, despite its weaknesses, the comparative analysis justifies consideration of this property for the World Heritage List, with the exclusion of the Big Solar Furnace (component part 2).

The boundaries of the nominated component parts and of the buffer zones are presented in the nomination dossier only through cartographic support, with limited explanation on the rationale for their delineation. The boundaries of the nominated component parts coincide either with cadastral plots or with the footprint of the nominated building. The boundaries of the buffer zones include immediately adjacent grounds, greenery, and, in some cases, other buildings or structures, and coincide with cadastral limits or morphological elements (e.g., street boundaries). In October 2025, the State Party explained that the nominated component parts are protected under Law no. 269-II/2001, which provides several layers of protection, including the possibility of establishing protection zones.

Additional information was received from the State Party on 26 February 2026. All additional information received has been incorporated into the relevant sections of this evaluation report. Date of ICOMOS approval of this report 11 March 2026 198 2 Description of the nominated property Note: The nomination dossier and additional information contain detailed descriptions of this property, its history and its state of conservation.

Integrity According to the nomination dossier, the nominated property retains integrity through its combined terrestrial and maritime landscape, which preserves key material and associative attributes of the Normandy Landings of 6 June 1944. These include representative elements of the Atlantic Wall in combat zones, remains of the logistical infrastructure of Operation Neptune, preserved wartime vestiges, memorial sites, and commemorative places established from 1944 onwards. The overall landscape integrity is considered satisfactory and continues to convey the operation's scale and unity. However, the condition and legibility of individual attributes vary due to post-war clearance activities, the recovery of materials, and natural coastal processes such as erosion and marine action, although sufficient elements remain to illustrate their historical significance.

The initial nominated property was both terrestrial and maritime, comprising a large sea area and eight contiguous land areas corresponding to the principal assault and defence sites. It covered approximately eighty kilometres of coastline identified by the Allies and divided into the five landing sectors: Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno and Sword, as well as Pointe du Hoc, the Longues-surMer battery, and the maritime remains of the Mulberry B artificial harbour and associated wrecks. The maritime area was defined as an archaeological field corresponding to the sea area visible from the terrestrial areas. A rather restricted buffer zone extended inland. However, this buffer zone did not extend into the maritime area, due to its vastness.

There are no permanent inhabitants in any of the component parts. The buffer zones of component parts 02 to 08 are scarcely populated (50-350 people on average), while the buffer zone of component part 01 is inhabited by approximately 17,000 people (2022 figures). The boundaries of the component parts have been largely aligned with cadastral plans and limits of protection under the historic monument designation; the latter have been extended in 2024 for component parts 02, 03, 07 and 08. The State Party has established the perimeters of the buffer zones based on a study of the immediate landscape setting of the component parts in relation to the existing protection framework, as well as a study of the wider landscape context, taking into consideration the surrounding territory within which the defence system functioned. This exercise allowed further to define the Landscape Influence Areas (AIP), where remotely located developments may still have some impacts on the nominated serial property, particularly the panoramas.

evidence.quote_en: The nominated extension was originally shortlisted as a potential component part in the 2008 nomination dossier, but ultimately it was not included in the nominated selection due to the process of privatisation of the cooperative buildings and state of conservation issues at the time. Since then, most of the apartment buildings have been acquired by a company with an interest in preserving the historical character of the estate. In addition, the issues relating to the state of conservation have been remedied as a result of the ongoing and comprehensive restoration efforts that have been undertaken. The State Party considers that Waldsiedlung Zehlendorf is now ready to be included in the Berlin Modernism Housing Estates, as a component part to complete the series.

Integrity The nominated extension was originally shortlisted as a potential component part in the 2008 nomination dossier, but ultimately it was not included in the nominated selection due to the process of privatisation of the cooperative buildings and state of conservation issues at the time. Since then, most of the apartment buildings have been acquired by a company with an interest in preserving the historical character of the estate. In addition, the issues relating to the state of conservation have been remedied as a result of the ongoing and comprehensive restoration efforts that have been undertaken. The State Party considers that Waldsiedlung Zehlendorf is now ready to be included in the Berlin Modernism Housing Estates, as a component part to complete the series.

There are 5,300 inhabitants in the proposed extension and 8,200 in the buffer zone (2024 figures). The boundaries of the nominated extension coincide with the historical limits of the Waldsiedlung Zehlendorf estate, which includes the single-family row houses, apartment buildings, shopping units, road network, the underground railway station with shopping arcades, and green spaces. A buffer zone has been established to provide an added layer of protection to the nominated extension. It encompasses the area where future developments could potentially threaten the significance and character of the nominated extension.

Recommendations with respect to inscription ICOMOS recommends that the extension of the Berlin Modernism Housing Estates to include Waldsiedlung Zehlendorf, Germany, be approved on the World Heritage List on the basis of criteria (ii) and (iv). Recommended Statement of Outstanding Universal Value Brief synthesis The set of housing estates in the Berlin Modern Style provides outstanding testimony to the implementation of housing policies during the period 1910-1933 and especially during the Weimar Republic, when the city of Berlin was characterized by its political, social, cultural and technical progressiveness. The housing estates reflect, with the highest degree of quality, the combination of urbanism, architecture, garden design and aesthetic research typical of early 20th century modernism, as well as the application of new hygienic and social standards.

It is also based on the capacity of the theatres to symbolise the "rubber boom" and to shape the urban landscape. Finally, the integrity of the nominated serial property also lies in the continuity of artistic functions combined with the participation of traditional cultural expressions and the creation of economic opportunities for the local population. As a serial nomination, integrity is also a measure of whether the component parts individually and collectively contain all the attributes necessary to express in a substantial way the proposed Outstanding Universal Value of the nominated property as a whole, and how each component part contributes to that value.

There are no permanent populations within the perimeter of the property. The population of the buffer zones could not be estimated by the State Party. The nominated theatres are the public property of the States of Pará and Amazonas, while the associated public spaces are the property of the cities of Belém and Manaus. The boundaries of component part 001, the Theatro da Paz and Praça da República, are bounded to the north by Rua Osvaldo Cruz, to the west by Avenida Presidente Vargas/Rua Arcipreste Manoel Teodoro, to the south by Rua Gama Abreu and Avenida Nossa Senhora de Nazaré, and to the east by Avenida Assis de Vasconcelos. Its buffer zone is delimited to the west by 292 the historic centre of the city, which includes the districts of Cidade Velha and Campina, and to the east by the first sectors of the expansion of the city along the main axis formed of wide, tree-lined avenues, the “boulevards”, such as Avenida Nossa Senhora de Nazaré.

Only thirty inhabitants live within the boundaries of the nominated property (2021 census). In the buffer zone there are about 10,400 inhabitants living in parts of eight settlements and one monastery (2021 census). The nominated property includes Mount Olympus and its wider area. The boundaries are based directly on the legal and management framework of the Mount Olympus National Park, established under Presidential Decree (Government Gazette 610/D’/2021), encompassing Zones A, B and C (the Nature Protection and National Park zones) while Zone D, the Regional Zone, falls primarily within the buffer zone. They also coincide with the limits of the area designated under the European Union’s Natura 2000 network as both a Special Area of Conservation and a Special Protection Area, named “Mount Olympus – Oros Olympos”.

ICOMOS recommends that the World Heritage Committee adopts the following draft recommendations, noting that this will be harmonised as appropriate with the draft recommendations of IUCN regarding their evaluation of this mixed property under the relevant criteria and included in the working document WHC/26/48.COM/8B. evidence.interpretation_note: Legal framework exists but is not yet fully implementable; decrees, registration/listing, and enhanced national protection remain unfinished.
Buffer-zone legal measures and urban regulations are projected rather than operational; added protection depends on future legal measures.
State of conservation is very fragile; no programmed conservation activities are underway; ICOMOS calls for urgent structured conservation planning.
Management is fragmented; roles, local authority involvement, formal/traditional integration and HIA procedures are not sufficiently clear.
ICOMOS states factors are not effectively managed because of lack of capacity, resources and an operational legal framework; no risk management plan exists yet.
Basic legal protection is in place, but boundaries must be refined and appended to the legal decree for effective protection and monitoring.
Buffer zones are explicitly not satisfactory in their protective role and must be revised and equipped with regulatory mechanisms.
Several structures are in critical condition; urgent operational recovery programmes, conservation strategy, stabilisation and shoring are required.
Key management elements exist but are not operationally clear; National Management Committee must be formally established with mandate and responsibilities.
Threats are identified, but response depends on resources; tourism is currently low, while inappropriate development and climate/coastal risks remain insufficiently controlled.
ICOMOS states the protection regime is adequate and will be strengthened by the future PSMV.
Buffer-zone controls exist through the 1985 decree, PAU and maritime/public-domain rules, but HIA should be formally embedded for projects in the property, buffer zone and setting.
There is no conservation programme in place; updated baseline documentation and a state of conservation assessment are urgently needed.
Management structures and plan exist, but coordination among INP, Management Unit and Steering Committee needs clarification; tourism and HIA tools need completion.
Landslide risk has reached alarming levels; disaster risk and emergency plans are urgent but not yet operational; tourism pressure also requires a strategy and carrying-capacity study.
Legal protection is described as established through national/provincial/municipal instruments; ICOMOS calls legal protection appropriate.
Component parts and buffer zones are incorporated into spatial planning; ICOMOS says buffer zones provide the necessary added layer of protection.
State of conservation is generally good and stable; documentation, conservation and monitoring are called adequate, relevant and robust.
ICOMOS states that a sound management system is in place, with community engagement, visitor management and interpretation.
Threats are well identified and responses exist, but ICOMOS recommends developing a risk preparedness plan and further capacity-building.
Legal protective measures are considered appropriate, although ICOMOS notes their focus is mainly on the built environment.
The single buffer zone connects the two component parts and partly denotes the broader sacred landscape, but wider sacred geography and surrounding development pressures require careful management.

evidence.summary_zh: Conservation measures and monitoring; report pp. 74-76
evidence.interpretation_note: Baseline documentation and monitoring are not adequate for all physical and intangible attributes; ICOMOS calls for detailed documentation and a monitoring system covering environmental and human factors.

evidence.summary_zh: Effectiveness of protection and management; report pp. 77-79
evidence.interpretation_note: Management exists but is complex across institutions and requires a more inclusive and participatory governance model, especially involving Buddhist stakeholders.

evidence.summary_zh: Factors affecting the nominated property; Monitoring; report pp. 70-76
evidence.interpretation_note: Development pressure, tourism, environmental factors and illegal construction are identified, but monitoring and response mechanisms are not adequate to address all threats.
The nominated series benefits from a solid legal and administrative protection framework supported by national heritage legislation, environmental regulation and national monument registration.

evidence.summary_zh: Conclusion; Protection and management; report pp. 89-93
evidence.interpretation_note: Protection is adequate, but ICOMOS considers minor buffer-zone adjustments desirable to ensure more coherent protection of the wider landscape setting.

evidence.summary_zh: State of conservation; Conservation measures; report pp. 83-89
evidence.interpretation_note: Extensive research, conservation and monitoring are in place, but the ruined fortifications remain vulnerable to erosion, vegetation, landslides and other natural processes.
A structured management plan with action plan, budget projections, indicators, implementation roadmaps and local management base provides an adequate basis for long-term conservation.
Environmental risks and socio-economic challenges are identified and risk strategies exist, but ICOMOS recommends using the World Heritage impact-assessment methodology for potential impacts.

evidence.summary_zh: Effectiveness of protection and management; report pp. 101-103
evidence.interpretation_note: ICOMOS explicitly considers the legal protection appropriate.
The protection and management systems are considered adequate and coordinated for the serial property, with no major buffer-zone deficiency carried into the final conclusion.

evidence.summary_zh: Conservation measures and monitoring; report pp. 99-101
evidence.interpretation_note: The nominated archaeological component parts are documented and managed under established protection and conservation arrangements.
A sound management system is in place, including visitor management and interpretation, but ICOMOS recommends further incorporation of residents and religious/spiritual communities.
Threats are addressed within an adequate management and coordination system; final concerns are mainly about broadening community participation rather than unmanaged risks.
ICOMOS considers legal protection to serve the safeguard of the nominated property.
No major buffer-zone inadequacy is retained in the final recommendation; protection is considered sufficient provided HIA is in place.

evidence.summary_zh: State of conservation; Factors affecting; report pp. 107-113
evidence.interpretation_note: Environmental vulnerability of soft limestone/chalk and pilgrimage pressure require continued conservation and monitoring, but these do not prevent inscription.
The management system can safeguard the property, but HIA, a comprehensive interpretation/presentation strategy and improved visitor/pilgrim facilities are needed.
Environmental risks, pollution, infrastructure and pilgrimage/tourism pressures are identified, but further HIA and visitor-management measures are required.
ICOMOS considers that each State Party has appropriate legal protection for component parts in its territory.

evidence.summary_zh: Protection and management; Effectiveness; report pp. 135-140
evidence.interpretation_note: Protection provisions exist for the component parts and their territories; the main shortcomings concern overall serial management and OUV logic rather than basic legal protection.

evidence.summary_zh: Conservation and monitoring; Effectiveness; report pp. 132-140
evidence.interpretation_note: Conservation systems exist at the national/component level but require stronger shared approaches, HIA and joint interpretation for the serial property.
No overarching management plan for the whole transnational serial property is yet in place, and some individual management plans still need completion.
Threats are managed at component/national level, but formal HIA processes and joint visitor/interpretation work are still needed as priorities.
ICOMOS considers the legal protection of the component parts adequate, supported by both formal protection and traditional reverence/taboos.
Basic protection is adequate, but any planned developments or activities around the component parts require Heritage Impact Assessment.
ICOMOS recommends developing a conservation strategy in line with international standards, including post-excavation treatment and presentation of tombs.
ICOMOS states that no management system for the nominated property is currently in place; the future administration would be created only after inscription and the management plan status is unclear.
The component parts are not yet prepared for increased visitors, visitor facilities are limited, and HIA is required for future projects.
ICOMOS considers legal protection adequate, supported by national law and traditional monastery protection.
The buffer and management arrangements are not identified as deficient; overall protection is considered adequate.

evidence.summary_zh: Conservation measures and monitoring; report pp. 159-161
evidence.interpretation_note: Documentation, monitoring and ongoing conservation are considered overall adequate and enhanced by traditional daily maintenance and formal monitoring.
ICOMOS considers the management system effective, with adequate visitor management and community involvement.
Development, disaster and visitation pressures are identified and managed within an adequate protection and management framework.
Component parts are legally protected, but implementation is weakened by unclear/insufficient regulatory regimes and agency capacity constraints.
Expanded buffer zones address ICOMOS concerns, but their effectiveness depends on approval, enforcement, cadastre/master-plan integration and temporary moratorium measures.
State of conservation is generally good but uneven; conservation and maintenance guidance/protocols are needed to avoid inappropriate interventions.
Governance is clear, but institutional cooperation should be formalised; monitoring, risk management, HIA and tourism management require further development.
Heavy urban development pressure and approved nearby projects remain a major risk until HIAs, buffer-zone regulations, visual protections and moratorium are operational.
Legal protection and current management frameworks are described as well-established and effective.

evidence.summary_zh: Factors affecting; Protection and management; report pp. 185-195
evidence.interpretation_note: Development pressures around proposed buffer zones and changes in ownership/function require continued coordination and planning attention.

evidence.summary_zh: State of conservation; Conservation measures; report pp. 185-191
evidence.interpretation_note: Conservation is characterised as sensitive, consistent and methodologically rigorous, supported by robust documentation and experienced stewardship.
Management frameworks are effective, but the proposed coordination mechanism requires further participatory development and implementation.
Climate change, development pressures and ownership/function changes are identified; comprehensive interpretation, presentation and visitor management are still needed.
Legal protection is adequate overall but does not always stem from heritage-specific provisions; ICOMOS recommends extending heritage protection to component parts not currently covered.
Some expanded buffer-zone areas are governed mainly by spatial/urban planning, so protection mechanisms within the buffer zone need strengthening.

evidence.summary_zh: State of conservation; Conservation measures; report pp. 200-207
evidence.interpretation_note: Material evidence survives and documentation is substantial, but coastal erosion, corrosion, cliff instability and uneven survival require continued conservation attention.
Governance is complex and requires strong coordination across instruments and stakeholders; interpretation also needs a coherent OUV-based strategy.
Coastal dynamics, renewable-energy projects, urban/tourism development and wind-farm impacts remain major issues requiring vigilance and strengthened planning controls.
Legal protection is appropriate but should be strengthened through local urban planning tools for all municipalities.
Buffer-zone/rural landscape management needs continued strengthening through local planning, fire prevention and landscape-management measures.

evidence.summary_zh: State of conservation; Factors affecting; report pp. 216-221
evidence.interpretation_note: Regular consolidation and restoration are ongoing, but several ruins remain unstable and environmental agents/fire/landslides require management.
The proposed future management system is well developed, but effectiveness depends on the capacity of the AMPM managing body.
Environmental factors, fire risk, tourism at Carcassonne and rural development pressures are identified and addressed, but tourism and interpretation strategies are still needed.
ICOMOS considers the nominated series to benefit from an appropriate level of legal protection and regional/local mechanisms safeguarding the attributes.

evidence.summary_zh: Protection and management; Effectiveness; report pp. 237-240
evidence.interpretation_note: Regional and local protection mechanisms are considered capable of safeguarding the attributes; no major buffer-zone deficiency is retained in the final conclusion.
Most auditoriums and key spaces are in very good or excellent condition, but some damage, technical systems and maintenance/restoration issues require continued attention.
A management framework exists, but the effectiveness of the new management body still depends on operational procedures and sustained long-term resources.

evidence.summary_zh: State of conservation; Effectiveness; report pp. 230-240
evidence.interpretation_note: Risks such as heating systems, wear, functional adaptations and resource sustainability are identified but require continued management and monitoring.
The nominated property has a robust legal protection framework and generally appropriate planning tools, but arrangements remain not fully operational.
Planning tools exist, but further coordination and World Heritage-specific impact-assessment methodologies are needed for the serial property and its settings.

The deferral recommendation calls for documenting attributes and conservation state of any selected component parts, indicating current conservation evidence is not yet sufficient for the revised serial approach.
The effectiveness of the management system is uncertain because it depends on formal establishment and operational capacity of AFAR and lacks some key stakeholders.
HIA, risk management, interpretation and visitor management still require strengthening; arrangements are not yet fully coordinated for long-term management.
ICOMOS considers the legal protection adequate; the non-inscription recommendation relates to OUV rather than protection failure.
The management and planning arrangements are considered adequate, with no major buffer-zone failure driving the recommendation.
The management plan and sustainable tourism plan provide relevant strategies and actions for conservation, safeguarding and presentation.
The management system is adequate, but formal Heritage Impact Assessment processes should be established.
Threats are addressed through relevant strategies, but formal HIA should be established for future impact control.
The property benefits from legal protection, an approved management plan, established institutional responsibilities and active conservation/documentation programmes.
Further strengthening is needed to control infrastructure impacts and visitor pressures in and around the site.
Active conservation and documentation programmes exist, but stronger integration and control of infrastructure/visitor impacts are needed.
Management exists but needs clearer coordination, decision-making arrangements and a more fully integrated system.
Visitor pressure, infrastructure impacts and community participation issues are recognised but require stronger control and integration.
ICOMOS considers legal protection of the nominated extension effective.

evidence.summary_zh: Protection and management; Effectiveness; report pp. 281-284
evidence.interpretation_note: Protection and management of the extension and its setting are adequate, with no outstanding buffer-zone concern in the final evaluation.

evidence.summary_zh: State of conservation; Conclusion; report pp. 276-284
evidence.interpretation_note: Restoration and conservation are well documented and implemented, with an adequate conservation plan and monitoring baseline.
ICOMOS considers the management system adequate; visitor management is satisfactory and communities are involved in decision-making.
Fire, climate and renewable-energy risks are identified and addressed through protection concepts and regulations.
The nominated property benefits from an excellent level of legal protection.
Urban transformation and high-rise/infrastructure projects in the buffer zone require adequate HIA and stronger control.

evidence.summary_zh: State of conservation; Factors affecting; report pp. 287-296
evidence.interpretation_note: Conservation of theatres is active and regularly inspected, but public-space use, overuse and excluded/weak components reveal continued conservation-management needs.
The envisaged management system and plan are likely to ensure preservation, but the Management Committee, future management plan and budgets still need to be created/approved within deadlines.
Fire, climate, invasive species, overcrowding and insufficiently controlled buffer-zone transformation are correctly identified but need stronger implementation and monitoring.
Protection and management are generally adequate and have proven efficient, but the Special Urban Plan must be completed and approved as a priority.
ICOMOS asks for revised/adjusted boundaries around Litochoro and operationalisation of the Special Urban Plan, indicating buffer/boundary protection is not yet sufficiently resolved.

evidence.summary_zh: State of conservation; Recommendations; report pp. 312-323
evidence.interpretation_note: Documentation of cultural attributes is not clear or systematic enough, and ICOMOS requests better maps, plans, photographs, models and scientific documentation.
The planned Coordinating Committee must be operationalised and management must better balance natural and cultural heritage, with stronger local participation.
Tourism, grazing, logging, hunting, flooding and other factors are identified, but integrated tourism strategy, carrying capacity and visitor monitoring are still required.

evidence.quote_en: ICOMOS considers that the comparative analysis justifies consideration of this property for the World Heritage List.
evidence.summary_zh: Comparative analysis 相关证据摘录。

evidence.quote_en: Criterion (iv) has been justified, while criterion (ii) is not demonstrated.
ICOMOS considers that the conditions of integrity are yet to be fully met.
ICOMOS considers that the authenticity of the nominated property has been demonstrated.
The nominated property boundaries still do not include all the evidence and supporting attributes of the modernist planning principles; the expanded buffer zone is welcomed and appears adequate.
ICOMOS considers that the documentation, conservation measures, and monitoring system for the nominated property are adequate.
evidence.summary_zh: Conservation and monitoring 相关证据摘录。

evidence.quote_en: ICOMOS considers that the management of the nominated property is satisfactory and is consistently implemented in a participatory manner.
evidence.summary_zh: Protection and management 相关证据摘录。

evidence.quote_en: Reviewing the state of the inner courtyards, whose auxiliary outbuildings make it difficult to perceive the original idea of open “green” spaces, and planning the removal of these auxiliary outbuildings accordingly,
ICOMOS considers that, despite substantial gaps and a certain lack of balance, the comparative analysis justifies consideration of this property for the World Heritage List.
evidence.summary_zh: Comparative analysis 相关证据摘录。

evidence.quote_en: Criterion (iii) is demonstrated, but criteria (ii) and (vi) are not demonstrated.
In the absence of a technical evaluation mission, ICOMOS has been unable to fully evaluate integrity; land expropriation plans make it highly vulnerable.
In the absence of a technical evaluation mission, ICOMOS has been unable to evaluate fully the authenticity of the nominated property.
The nominated property boundary is logical, but the basis and suitability of the buffer zone could not be fully evaluated.
Conservation initiatives are hindered by governance challenges; a holistic approach is currently impossible to implement and monitoring requires strengthening.
evidence.summary_zh: Conservation and monitoring 相关证据摘录。

evidence.quote_en: The separation of administrative authority and the ongoing conflict hinder holistic protection and management and make the system not fully operational.
evidence.summary_zh: Protection and management 相关证据摘录。

evidence.quote_en: ICOMOS considers that documented, imminent expropriation and land-seizure measures constitute an emergency situation and threaten the integrity of the property.
evidence.summary_zh: Emergency threats 相关证据摘录。

evidence.quote_en: ICOMOS recommends that a Reactive Monitoring mission be invited to the property, if this is feasible, to establish a Desired state of conservation and a programme of corrective measures to remove the property from the List of World Heritage in Danger.
evidence.interpretation_note: 反应性监测任务建议。

merge_metadata.colleague_workbook: /Users/weiq/Documents/Codex/48 届世界遗产大会观察/同事工作成果/@文化遗产申报项目信息填表--0709.xlsx
notes: C1750 recommendation count was verified against the report as 7 (a-g), despite the manual note stating 10.
C1809 report-derived four-level ratings remain draft pending human review.
